title: Chapter 9: The Crackdown
content: Nurse Cross struck back on day fifteen.
The Manifesto had been circulating for three days. The administration had noticed. And now they responded.
At 6:00 AM, an announcement came over the intercom:
*"Effective immediately, all patients are restricted to their assigned wards. Recreational Period is suspended until further notice. Room inspections will be conducted twice daily."*
Chloe sat up in bed and listened to the silence that followed.
Then she heard someone down the hall start laughing.
It was Sarah.
"They are scared," Sarah called out. "They would not do this if they were not scared."
Chloe smiled. Sarah had found her voice.
At 9:00 AM, the room inspections began.
Two orderlies entered Chloe's room without knocking. They flipped her mattress. Opened every drawer. Checked the floorboards.
They did not find the camera. It was in the ceiling panel above the bathroom, taped to the back of the ventilation grate.
They did not find the SD card. It was inside the hollowed-out spine of a paperback romance novel on her shelf.
They did not find the burner phone. Mia had it.
"Clean room," one orderly said.
"I know," Chloe replied. "I keep it tidy."
At lunch, the dining hall was quiet. The usual conversations had been replaced by the silence of people who knew they were being watched.
Chloe sat at her usual table. Mia slid in across from her. Tiffany sat beside her, picking at her food but at least eating.
"They are tightening the net," Mia said quietly. "I heard they are bringing in a new patient tonight. High-profile. Someone's daughter."
"Whose?"
"I do not know. But the admin office has been preparing her intake file for a week. Extra precautions. A private room on the third floor."
"Third floor is for special cases," Chloe said.
"Exactly."
Chloe looked at her food. The soup had gone cold. She pushed it away.
"We need to move faster."
"We need to be careful."
"Careful is what got us here. Everyone was too careful. Too compliant. Too scared to push back."
Mia met her eyes. "What are you proposing?"
"The camera. I have been taking photos. But I need to show people what I have captured. Not just tell them."
"That is how you get caught."
"That is how you start a riot."
The crackdown intensified.
By day seventeen, the facility had implemented:

- Random urine tests to check if patients were skipping medication
- A ban on all personal items not issued by the facility
- Confiscation of all books except the Bible and approved self-help texts
- A curfew that required patients to be in bed by 8:00 PM
The women of Ward B responded with quiet, coordinated resistance.
When orderlies came for the books, Denise handed over a copy of *The Power of Now* and hid her collection of feminist essays in the ceiling tiles.
When they demanded urine samples, Sarah drank so much water before her test that the sample came back too diluted to analyze.

When the 8:00 PM curfew started, the women began a ritual. At exactly 7:59, every patient in Ward B would open their door and stand in the threshold for exactly thirty seconds. Not leaving. Just standing. A silent demonstration.
The orderlies did not know what to do. They could not punish patients for standing in their own doorways.
Nurse Cross knew exactly what was happening. But she could not prove Chloe was behind it.
"The standing protest is beautiful," Mia said on the night of day eighteen. They were huddled in the bathroom, the router's green light their only illumination.
"It was Sarah's idea."
"Sarah is becoming dangerous."
"Good."

Mia turned the tablet. "I have finished mapping the hidden server. The data it sends every night — it is not patient records. It is a log of every conversation the microphones pick up. Every word spoken in every room."
Chloe felt cold. "They are recording everything we say?"
"Everything. The cameras are for show. The microphones are the real surveillance."
"And my father's firm receives these transcripts?"
"Every morning at 2:00 AM."
Chloe sat back against the tile wall. Her father had been reading her therapy sessions. Her conversations with Mia. Her jokes with Tiffany.
"He knows everything I have said in here."
"He knows everything anyone has said."
"Does he know about the camera?"
"If you did not talk about it near a microphone, no."
Chloe thought back. She had never discussed the camera out loud. She had only written notes.
For once, her paranoia had paid off.
"We need to act before the new patient arrives," Chloe said. "If they are bringing someone with a private room, it means something is changing. We need to be ready."
"Ready for what?"
Chloe looked at the router's blinking light. "To show everyone what this place really is."
"That will end us."
"Or it will free us."
Mia was silent for a long moment. Then she said: "I will have the file ready by tomorrow night."
"That is fast."
"You said we needed to move faster."
The new patient arrived at 8:00 PM.

Chloe watched from her window as a black SUV pulled through the gates. The woman who stepped out was young — early twenties, like Chloe. She wore street clothes, not a uniform. She was not crying. She was not fighting.
She looked around the courtyard with the calm, assessing gaze of someone who was mapping exits.
Chloe recognized that look.
It was the same look she had worn on day one.
The next morning, Chloe found a note slipped under her door.
It was on expensive paper. The handwriting was elegant. Precise.
*"I hear you are running a black market. I want in. — Room 301"*
Chloe read the note three times.
Then she slipped it into her pocket and walked to Room 301.
Room 301 was at the end of the third-floor corridor. A private room. The door was unlocked.
Chloe knocked.
"Come in."
The woman from the black SUV was sitting on the bed, legs crossed, phone in hand. Not a burner. Her actual phone. She still had it.
"You must be Chloe."
"And you are?"
"Amara." She held out her hand. "Amara Osei. My father is a senator. He sent me here because I refused to endorse his re-election campaign publicly."
Chloe shook her hand slowly. "You still have your phone."
"They tried to take it. I told them my father would sue the facility into the ground if they confiscated senator's daughter's property without a court order. They backed down."
"That is the most beautiful thing I have heard since arriving."
Amara smiled. "I heard you have been running a resistance movement from Ward B. I want to help."
"Why?"
"Because my father is going to be very embarrassed when I expose this place. And I want front-row seats."
Chloe looked at the phone in Amara's hand. At the private room. At the woman who had walked in with more power than any patient in this facility.
"How fast can you copy files?"
"Very."
She walked back to her room with a new phone number in her pocket and a new ally in the building.
The resistance had just gained a senator's daughter.
Nurse Cross had no idea what was coming.
